and then slows down and then once we get to about March and Easter it drops off and we've got a dip during the middle of the year. We've got that same pattern for 11 years of data. How much would you cheerfully pay to get a new customer? Obviously that's going to depend hugely on what you're selling but also on your business model. Are you expecting to sell to this person over and over again? Or are you one and done? You know if you're selling a 30 grand garage or a shed just to go down the bottom of the garden or something like that then you're probably not expecting a lot of repeat custom in which case that first transaction had better be profitable. But if you're selling young woman's fast fashion and your average customer comes back to you four times a year and some people come back to you 15 times a year then you don't need to make as much money on that first sale. So you've got to be very very clear on what the goal is here. To know that you've got to know your break-even point at which point how much money do I have to spend acquiring a new lead or acquiring a sale where I make zero dollars and is that then different from what we're aiming for? So by that I mean let's say my break-even is a hundred dollars. So as soon as I've spent a hundred dollars acquiring let's say a lead I know that I'm not making any money on the sale. But what should my target CPA be? Again it depends on so CPA being cost per action. My target in other words what I want to go get leads for will be probably somewhere between zero and a hundred if my break-even is a hundred. Sometimes it might be a little bit higher you know you've probably heard the expression of a lost leader and that's where you might go backwards a little bit on the initial sale because you know you're going to make it up later. We've got a client that sells vitamins. Now for them it's worth spending a bit of money on the front end and paying a bit to buy a new customer because they know that their average customer is going to come back and order month after month after month after month. got time to talk about match types but i thought it's worth doing there are these things called match types at the top here we have exact match donated by the square brackets so the left hand side is the keyword the right hand side is the search query just as we had it before then we have things like phrase match which is putting the double quotes around the keyword so exact match only matches and there are tiny little areas where this is not the case but for the most part only matches the exact search query so it matches the exact hence exact match so in other words if your keyword is an exact match keyword it's quite limiting but it's also quite safe you know exactly who's going to see your ad only those people that have typed in those two words and only those two words in that order they're the only people that are going to see your ad now if you move to phrase match you're expanding out the universe of people that you can show ads to a little bit so by targeting the phrase samsung tv you're saying that these two words have to be in the search query in that order with nothing in between them but there can be other words before

## [36:33 - 39:38]

and or after so best samsung tv samsung tv deal samsung tv near me but not samsung red tv because that doesn't include the phrase samsung tv so you start to broaden it out a little bit but you also start to get the odd few weird search queries and you start to potentially show ads to people that you don't really want to show ads to but it's safer than what's called a modified broad match term which basically says to google by having the plus signs in your keyword it says these words have to be in the search query somewhere but as long as they're in there anywhere i don't really care and so that's really broadening it out and then you have this thing called a broad match keyword which basically says if they're vaguely connected if the search query and the keyword are vaguely connected then show my ad i'm going to suggest for your very very first google ads account you stick to exact match yes you're going to limit the number of people that you can show ads to but if you can't make that profitable then you ain't going to make broad match profitable for the most part i advise staying well away from broad match for your first sort of few months maybe even your first year of playing with google ads there are certainly cases where you want to use phrase and modified broad but for now just stick to exact match so use the square brackets or find the little drop down in your google ads account next to the keyword and choose exact match when you're creating the keywords in your account and the last thing we need to cover with keywords is this thing called a negative keyword and negative keywords or necks stop your ads appearing so in other words if my negative keyword is free and the search query is free samsung tv then i'm telling google if this negative keyword is in that search query somewhere i don't want to show this person the ad so if you only sold new tv's then things like second hand or vintage would be good negative keywords now you don't need to worry about necks if you do follow my advice and only use exact match keywords because you're not going to run into trouble with exact match keywords but once you move on to phrase and modified broad ## [57:53 - 1:00:56]

the machine needs data in order to play with that in order to try and figure out how much it should be bidding for every click so start with manual bidding you can always move to one of the more advanced automated strategies later on you can if you want set an end date something that we almost never use but if you know that this campaign is only going to run for a set amount of time or you want all these ads turned off after black friday and you never want to run this campaign again then you can set an end date and you can ignore dsa or dynamic search and additional settings you can ignore those for now so we've got a campaign set up now we need to go build some ad groups and i said i'd give you a different way of visualizing this a different way of picturing this so the way i've always thought of ad groups is that they're buckets and a bucket contains a bunch of keywords remember we said generally it's probably going to be between about one to ten keywords when we're starting out and on the front of that bucket is slapped on an ad or two or three ideally at least two ads and no more than four for every one of our buckets for every one of our ad groups and what that means is that every time someone searches for a search query that matches one of our keywords they're going to see at random one of the ads on the front of our bucket obviously they're not going to see both ads they're only going to see one and over time we're going to get to see the stats of which ad performed best now this is the wrong way to set up a google ads account to have one ad group in your campaign with loads and loads and loads and loads of keywords in that one bucket because again remember if the search query matches the keyword that's in that bucket they're going to randomly see one of the ads that slapped on the front of that bucket so if you do it this way it basically means you have no control i've seen accounts with 300 keywords in an ad group and what you're saying to the machine is if any of those 300 pop up if someone's searching for something that matches any one of those 300 keywords then show the ad on the front of the bucket now the very very unlikely that that ad is going to be as relevant as it could be to all 300 of those terms think of it as 300 different people 300 different people searching maybe only slightly differently but they're searching differently do they all really want to see the same ad instead what we want to do is to theme our keywords now when we talked about research we've covered that so you've already done that the way you've done your research is by thinking in terms of themes now we're just simply going to turn each of your themes into different ad groups if you want to be really really granular with this some setups and we'll typically use this although that's changing a little over

time but for the last few years we have done this where each ad group will only contain one keyword now that means i've got to do more work at the beginning to write more ads because instead of three to the search that that person just did but don't do that when you're starting out that's too much work you're going to start obsessing and you're going to take days and days over the setup trying to write 60 ads across 30 ad groups just don't do that at the beginning start with just one ad group for each theme write a couple of ads and remember one to ten keywords in each theme and then just as a rough rule of thumb can i fit all of those all of the different words in my keyword list can i fit all of those individual words into the one ad so if i had say samsung tv cost and samsung tv price they're similar enough that they can go in the same ad group when you're starting out but samsung tv remote control if you sell remote controls that belongs in its own remote control ad group so that
